text: The surgeon mobilizes and sections the ileostomy using a linear stapler, then places it back into the peritoneal cavity with a single port device. The last ileal loop is freed from adhesions, and the superior mesenteric pedicle is mobilized to allow the loop to reach the pelvis without tension. A J-pouch is constructed with two fires of a 100mm linear stapler. A patency test with saline is performed, and the anvil of a 31mm circular stapler is secured at the tip of the pouch with a 0-prolene purse string. Fluorescence angiography confirms bowel vascularization, and a 19 French tubular drain is placed at the tip of the anvil.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection of the rectum down to the levator muscle plane, detaching the tunica muscularis propria from the pelvic floor's striated muscle. A lone star retractor and semicircular anoscope are used transanally to expose the anal canal, dentate line, and distal rectum. Anterior transillumination aids in visualizing the dissection level and confirms complete mobilization of the rectal wall. A zero-proline suture purse string is placed at the distal rectum, with full thickness bites checked from both transanal and laparoscopic views, ensuring no surrounding structures are included. The purse string is then securely tightened.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to perform a rectotomy close to the purse string folds, preserving the rectal cuff length. Electrocautery marks the line of transaction, which progresses until entering the pelvic cavity. Laparoscopic transillumination from above guides the transanal transaction, ensuring no injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon extracts the specimen transanally, revealing a homogeneous rectal cuff with clear margins using a circular anoscope. A purse ring with a zero prurine suture is placed on the rectal cuff, ensuring safety as the rectal wall is completely dissected from surrounding structures. The drainage at the tip of the anvil is pulled through the anus, and the purse ring is tightened around the anvil after confirming the pouch's correct orientation. A standard millimeter circular stapler is approximated with the anvil to create a double purse string single stapled anastomosis. The anastomosis is inspected for defects, and fluorescence angiography confirms optimal vascularization of the mucosal side. A reverse air leak test is performed to check for non-visible leaks.
